text: Wedlock suits you, I think, Watson, that you have put on seven and a half pounds since I saw you.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating Irene Adler and the compromising photograph.

text: It is simplicity itself, my eyes tell me that on the inside of your left shoe, just where the firelight strikes it, the leather is scored by six almost parallel cuts. Obviously they have been caused by someone who has very carelessly scraped round the edges of the sole in order to remove crusted mud from it. Hence, you see, my double deduction that you had been out in vile weather, and that you had a particularly malignant boot-slitting specimen of the London slavey. As to your practice, if a gentleman walks into my rooms smelling of iodoform, with a black mark of nitrate of silver upon his right forefinger, and a bulge on the right side of his top-hat to show where he has secreted his stethoscope, I must be dull, indeed, if I do not pronounce him to be an active member of the medical profession.
context: Holmes comments on Irene Adler and the compromising photograph during the investigation.

text: Quite so, You see, but you do not observe. The distinction is clear. For example, you have frequently seen the steps which lead up from the hall to this room.
context: Holmes comments on Irene Adler and the compromising photograph during the investigation.

text: Quite so! You have not observed. And yet you have seen. That is just my point. Now, I know that there are seventeen steps, because I have both seen and observed.
context: Holmes comments on Irene Adler and the compromising photograph during the investigation.

text: I have no data yet. It is a capital mistake to theorise before one has data. Insensibly one begins to twist facts to suit theories, instead of theories to suit facts. But the note itself. What do you deduce from it?
context: Holmes comments on Irene Adler and the compromising photograph during the investigation.

text: Peculiar—that is the very word, It is not an English paper at all. Hold it up to the light.
context: Holmes comments on Irene Adler and the compromising photograph during the investigation.

text: Pray take a seat, This is my friend and colleague, Dr. Watson, who is occasionally good enough to help me in my cases. Whom have I the honour to address?
context: Holmes addresses a client while investigating Irene Adler and the compromising photograph.

text: Why, indeed? Your Majesty had not spoken before I was aware that I was addressing Wilhelm Gottsreich Sigismond von Ormstein, Grand Duke of Cassel-Felstein, and hereditary King of Bohemia.
context: Holmes addresses the King of Bohemia while investigating Irene Adler and the compromising photograph.

text: Let me see! Hum! Born in New Jersey in the year 1858. Contralto—hum! La Scala, hum!
context: Holmes addresses the King of Bohemia while investigating Irene Adler and the compromising photograph.

text: Prima donna Imperial Opera of Warsaw—yes! Retired from operatic stage—ha! Living in London—quite so! Your Majesty, as I understand, became entangled with this young person, wrote her some compromising letters, and is now desirous of getting those letters back.
context: Holmes addresses the King of Bohemia while investigating Irene Adler and the compromising photograph.

text: Oh, then we have three days yet, That is very fortunate, as I have one or two matters of importance to look into just at present. Your Majesty will, of course, stay in London for the present?
context: Holmes addresses the King of Bohemia while investigating Irene Adler and the compromising photograph.

text: You see, this marriage rather simplifies matters. The photograph becomes a double-edged weapon now. The chances are that she would be as averse to its being seen by Mr. Godfrey Norton, as our client is to its coming to the eyes of his princess. Now the question is, Where are we to find the photograph?
context: Holmes comments on Irene Adler and the compromising photograph during the investigation.

text: It was all-important. When a woman thinks that her house is on fire, her instinct is at once to rush to the thing which she values most. It is a perfectly overpowering impulse, and I have more than once taken advantage of it. In the case of the Darlington Substitution Scandal it was of use to me, and also in the Arnsworth Castle business. A married woman grabs at her baby; an unmarried one reaches for her jewel-box.
context: Holmes comments on Irene Adler and the compromising photograph during the investigation.

text: Now it was clear to me that our lady of to-day had nothing in the house more precious to her than what we are in quest of. She would rush to secure it. The alarm of fire was admirably done. The smoke and shouting were enough to shake nerves of steel. She responded beautifully.
context: Holmes comments on Irene Adler and the compromising photograph during the investigation.

text: The photograph is in a recess behind a sliding panel just above the right bell-pull. She was there in an instant, and I caught a glimpse of it as she half drew it out. When I cried out that it was a false alarm, she replaced it, glanced at the rocket, rushed from the room, and I have not seen her since. I rose, and, making my excuses, escaped from the house. I hesitated whether to attempt to secure the photograph at once; but the coachman had come in, and as he was watching me narrowly, it seemed safer to wait.
context: Holmes comments on Irene Adler and the compromising photograph during the investigation.

text: I've heard that voice before, Now, I wonder who the deuce that could have been.
context: Holmes comments on Irene Adler and the compromising photograph during the investigation.

text: From what I have seen of the lady, she seems, indeed, to be on a very different level to your Majesty, I am sorry that I have not been able to bring your Majesty's business to a more successful conclusion.
context: Holmes addresses the King of Bohemia while investigating Irene Adler and the compromising photograph.

text: Try the settee, I know, my dear Watson, that you share my love of all that is bizarre and outside the conventions and humdrum routine of everyday life. You have shown your relish for it by the enthusiasm which has prompted you to chronicle, and, if you will excuse my saying so, somewhat to embellish so many of my own little adventures.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating Jabez Wilson's unusual league.

text: I begin to think, Watson, that I make a mistake in explaining. '_Omne ignotum pro magnifico_,' you know, and my poor little reputation, such as it is, will suffer shipwreck if I am so candid. Can you not find the advertisement, Mr. Wilson?
context: Holmes addresses Watson and Jabez Wilson while investigating Jabez Wilson's unusual league.

text: It is a little off the beaten track, isn't it?
context: Holmes comments on Jabez Wilson's unusual league during the investigation.

text: Your experience has been a most entertaining one, Pray continue your very interesting statement.
context: Holmes comments on Jabez Wilson's unusual league during the investigation.

text: No, no, I really wouldn't miss your case for the world. It is most refreshingly unusual. But there is, if you will excuse my saying so, something just a little funny about it. Pray what steps did you take when you found the card upon the door?
context: Holmes comments on Jabez Wilson's unusual league during the investigation.

text: And you did very wisely, Your case is an exceedingly remarkable one, and I shall be happy to look into it. From what you have told me I think that it is possible that graver issues hang from it than might at first sight appear.
context: Holmes comments on Jabez Wilson's unusual league during the investigation.

text: As far as you are personally concerned, I do not see that you have any grievance against this extraordinary league. On the contrary, you are, as I understand, richer by some £ 30, to say nothing of the minute knowledge which you have gained on every subject which comes under the letter A. You have lost nothing by them.
context: Holmes comments on Jabez Wilson's unusual league during the investigation.

text: Well, Watson, what do you make of it all?
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating Jabez Wilson's unusual league.

text: As a rule, the more bizarre a thing is the less mysterious it proves to be. It is your commonplace, featureless crimes which are really puzzling, just as a commonplace face is the most difficult to identify. But I must be prompt over this matter.
context: Holmes comments on Jabez Wilson's unusual league during the investigation.

text: Thank you, I only wished to ask you how you would go from here to the Strand.
context: Holmes comments on Jabez Wilson's unusual league during the investigation.

text: Smart fellow, that, He is, in my judgment, the fourth smartest man in London, and for daring I am not sure that he has not a claim to be third. I have known something of him before.
context: Holmes comments on Jabez Wilson's unusual league during the investigation.

text: Let me see, I should like just to remember the order of the houses here. It is a hobby of mine to have an exact knowledge of London. There is Mortimer's, the tobacconist, the little newspaper shop, the Coburg branch of the City and Suburban Bank, the Vegetarian Restaurant, and McFarlane's carriage-building depot. That carries us right on to the other block. And now, Doctor, we've done our work, so it's time we had some play.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating Jabez Wilson's unusual league.

text: A sandwich and a cup of coffee, and then off to violin-land, where all is sweetness and delicacy and harmony, and there are no red-headed clients to vex us with their conundrums.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating Jabez Wilson's unusual league.

text: Ha! Our party is complete, Watson, I think you know Mr. Jones, of Scotland Yard? Let me introduce you to Mr. Merryweather, who is to be our companion in to-night's adventure.
context: Holmes addresses Watson and Inspector Jones while investigating Jabez Wilson's unusual league.

text: I think you will find, that you will play for a higher stake to-night than you have ever done yet, and that the play will be more exciting. For you, Mr. Merryweather, the stake will be some £ 30,000; and for you, Jones, it will be the man upon whom you wish to lay your hands.
context: Holmes addresses Inspector Jones and Mr. Merryweather while investigating Jabez Wilson's unusual league.

text: We are close there now, This fellow Merryweather is a bank director, and personally interested in the matter. I thought it as well to have Jones with us also. He is not a bad fellow, though an absolute imbecile in his profession. He has one positive virtue. He is as brave as a bulldog and as tenacious as a lobster if he gets his claws upon anyone.
context: Holmes comments on Jabez Wilson's unusual league during the investigation.

text: I must really ask you to be a little more quiet! You have already imperilled the whole success of our expedition. Might I beg that you would have the goodness to sit down upon one of those boxes, and not to interfere?
context: Holmes comments on Jabez Wilson's unusual league during the investigation.

text: Which were very well justified, And now it is time that we arranged our little plans. I expect that within an hour matters will come to a head. In the meantime Mr. Merryweather, we must put the screen over that dark lantern.
context: Holmes addresses Mr. Merryweather while investigating Jabez Wilson's unusual league.

text: They have but one retreat, That is back through the house into Saxe-Coburg Square. I hope that you have done what I asked you, Jones?
context: Holmes addresses Inspector Jones while investigating Jabez Wilson's unusual league.

text: It's no use, John Clay, You have no chance at all.
context: Holmes addresses John Clay while investigating Jabez Wilson's unusual league.

text: And I you, Your red-headed idea was very new and effective.
context: Holmes comments on Jabez Wilson's unusual league during the investigation.

text: I have had one or two little scores of my own to settle with Mr. John Clay, I have been at some small expense over this matter, which I shall expect the bank to refund, but beyond that I am amply repaid by having had an experience which is in many ways unique, and by hearing the very remarkable narrative of the Red-headed League.
context: Holmes addresses John Clay while investigating Jabez Wilson's unusual league.

text: My dear fellow, life is infinitely stranger than anything which the mind of man could invent. We would not dare to conceive the things which are really mere commonplaces of existence. If we could fly out of that window hand in hand, hover over this great city, gently remove the roofs, and peep in at the queer things which are going on, the strange coincidences, the plannings, the cross-purposes, the wonderful chains of events, working through generations, and leading to the most _outré_ results, it would make all fiction with its conventionalities and foreseen conclusions most stale and unprofitable.
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: A certain selection and discretion must be used in producing a realistic effect, This is wanting in the police report, where more stress is laid, perhaps, upon the platitudes of the magistrate than upon the details, which to an observer contain the vital essence of the whole matter. Depend upon it, there is nothing so unnatural as the commonplace.
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: Indeed, your example is an unfortunate one for your argument, This is the Dundas separation case, and, as it happens, I was engaged in clearing up some small points in connection with it. The husband was a teetotaler, there was no other woman, and the conduct complained of was that he had drifted into the habit of winding up every meal by taking out his false teeth and hurling them at his wife, which, you will allow, is not an action likely to occur to the imagination of the average story-teller. Take a pinch of snuff, Doctor, and acknowledge that I have scored over you in your example.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé.

text: I have seen those symptoms before, Oscillation upon the pavement always means an _affaire de cœur_. She would like advice, but is not sure that the matter is not too delicate for communication. And yet even here we may discriminate. When a woman has been seriously wronged by a man she no longer oscillates, and the usual symptom is a broken bell wire. Here we may take it that there is a love matter, but that the maiden is not so much angry as perplexed, or grieved.
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: But here she comes in person to resolve our doubts.
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: Never mind, it is my business to know things. Perhaps I have trained myself to see what others overlook. If not, why should you come to consult me?
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: Why did you come away to consult me in such a hurry?
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: Your father, your stepfather, surely, since the name is different.
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: You interest me extremely, And since you draw so large a sum as a hundred a year, with what you earn into the bargain, you no doubt travel a little and indulge yourself in every way. I believe that a single lady can get on very nicely upon an income of about £ 60.
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: You have made your position very clear to me, This is my friend, Dr. Watson, before whom you can speak as freely as before myself. Kindly tell us now all about your connection with Mr. Hosmer Angel.
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: I suppose, that when Mr. Windibank came back from France he was very annoyed at your having gone to the ball.
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: It was most suggestive, It has long been an axiom of mine that the little things are infinitely the most important. Can you remember any other little things about Mr. Hosmer Angel?
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: I shall glance into the case for you, and I have no doubt that we shall reach some definite result. Let the weight of the matter rest upon me now, and do not let your mind dwell upon it further. Above all, try to let Mr. Hosmer Angel vanish from your memory, as he has done from your life.
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: This is the girl's stepfather, Mr. James Windibank, He has written to me to say that he would be here at six. Come in!
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: Good-evening, Mr. James Windibank, I think that this typewritten letter is from you, in which you made an appointment with me for six o'clock?
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: On the contrary, I have every reason to believe that I will succeed in discovering Mr. Hosmer Angel.
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: It is a curious thing, that a typewriter has really quite as much individuality as a man's handwriting. Unless they are quite new, no two of them write exactly alike. Some letters get more worn than others, and some wear only on one side. Now, you remark in this note of yours, Mr. Windibank, that in every case there is some little slurring over of the 'e,' and a slight defect in the tail of the 'r.' There are fourteen other characteristics, but those are the more obvious.
context: Holmes addresses James Windibank while investigating Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé.

text: And now I will show you what is really a very interesting study, Mr. Windibank, I think of writing another little monograph some of these days on the typewriter and its relation to crime. It is a subject to which I have devoted some little attention. I have here four letters which purport to come from the missing man. They are all typewritten. In each case, not only are the 'e's' slurred and the 'r's' tailless, but you will observe, if you care to use my magnifying lens, that the fourteen other characteristics to which I have alluded are there as well.
context: Holmes addresses James Windibank while investigating Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé.

text: Certainly, I let you know, then, that I have caught him!
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: Oh, it won't do—really it won't, There is no possible getting out of it, Mr. Windibank. It is quite too transparent, and it was a very bad compliment when you said that it was impossible for me to solve so simple a question. That's right! Sit down and let us talk it over.
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: The law cannot, as you say, touch you, yet there never was a man who deserved punishment more. If the young lady has a brother or a friend, he ought to lay a whip across your shoulders. By Jove!
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: There's a cold-blooded scoundrel! That fellow will rise from crime to crime until he does something very bad, and ends on a gallows. The case has, in some respects, been not entirely devoid of interest.
context: Holmes comments on Mary Sutherland's missing fiancé during the investigation.

text: But it is profoundly true. Singularity is almost invariably a clue. The more featureless and commonplace a crime is, the more difficult it is to bring it home. In this case, however, they have established a very serious case against the son of the murdered man.
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: Circumstantial evidence is a very tricky thing, It may seem to point very straight to one thing, but if you shift your own point of view a little, you may find it pointing in an equally uncompromising manner to something entirely different. It must be confessed, however, that the case looks exceedingly grave against the young man, and it is very possible that he is indeed the culprit. There are several people in the neighbourhood, however, and among them Miss Turner, the daughter of the neighbouring landowner, who believe in his innocence, and who have retained Lestrade, whom you may recollect in connection with the Study in Scarlet, to work out the case in his interest. Lestrade, being rather puzzled, has referred the case to me, and hence it is that two middle-aged gentlemen are flying westward at fifty miles an hour instead of quietly digesting their breakfasts at home.
context: Holmes addresses Inspector Lestrade while investigating the Boscombe Valley death.

text: There is nothing more deceptive than an obvious fact, Besides, we may chance to hit upon some other obvious facts which may have been by no means obvious to Mr. Lestrade. You know me too well to think that I am boasting when I say that I shall either confirm or destroy his theory by means which he is quite incapable of employing, or even of understanding. To take the first example to hand, I very clearly perceive that in your bedroom the window is upon the right-hand side, and yet I question whether Mr. Lestrade would have noted even so self-evident a thing as that.
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: My dear fellow, I know you well. I know the military neatness which characterises you. You shave every morning, and in this season you shave by the sunlight; but since your shaving is less and less complete as we get farther back on the left side, until it becomes positively slovenly as we get round the angle of the jaw, it is surely very clear that that side is less illuminated than the other. I could not imagine a man of your habits looking at himself in an equal light and being satisfied with such a result. I only quote this as a trivial example of observation and inference.
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: On the contrary, it is the brightest rift which I can at present see in the clouds. However innocent he might be, he could not be such an absolute imbecile as not to see that the circumstances were very black against him. Had he appeared surprised at his own arrest, or feigned indignation at it, I should have looked upon it as highly suspicious, because such surprise or anger would not be natural under the circumstances, and yet might appear to be the best policy to a scheming man. His frank acceptance of the situation marks him as either an innocent man, or else as a man of considerable self-restraint and firmness. As to his remark about his deserts, it was also not unnatural if you consider that he stood beside the dead body of his father, and that there is no doubt that he had that very day so far forgotten his filial duty as to bandy words with him, and even, according to the little girl whose evidence is so important, to raise his hand as if to strike him.
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: The self-reproach and contrition which are displayed in his remark appear to me to be the signs of a healthy mind rather than of a guilty one.
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: It was very nice and complimentary of you, It is entirely a question of barometric pressure.
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: I hope we may clear him, Miss Turner, You may rely upon my doing all that I can.
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: And your father? Was he in favour of such a union?
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: I think that I see my way to clearing James McCarthy, Have you an order to see him in prison?
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: You are right, you do find it very hard to tackle the facts.
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: Well, moonshine is a brighter thing than fog, But I am very much mistaken if this is not Hatherley Farm upon the left.
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: _Nous verrons_, You work your own method, and I shall work mine. I shall be busy this afternoon, and shall probably return to London by the evening train.
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: All right, I have given you the chance. Here are your lodgings. Good-bye. I shall drop you a line before I leave.
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: Pray sit down on the sofa, You had my note?
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: Well, it is not for me to judge you, I pray that we may never be exposed to such a temptation.
context: Holmes comments on the Boscombe Valley death during the investigation.

text: Give me your coat and umbrella, They may rest here on the hook and will be dry presently. You have come up from the south-west, I see.
context: Holmes comments on the threatening orange pips during the investigation.

text: You fill me with interest, Pray give us the essential facts from the commencement, and I can afterwards question you as to those details which seem to me to be most important.
context: Holmes comments on the threatening orange pips during the investigation.

text: One moment, your statement is, I foresee, one of the most remarkable to which I have ever listened. Let me have the date of the reception by your uncle of the letter, and the date of his supposed suicide.
context: Holmes comments on the threatening orange pips during the investigation.

text: Tut! tut! You must act, man, or you are lost. Nothing but energy can save you. This is no time for despair.
context: Holmes comments on the threatening orange pips during the investigation.

text: Thank you! And now you must on no account lose another instant. We cannot spare time even to discuss what you have told me. You must get home instantly and act.
context: Holmes comments on the threatening orange pips during the investigation.

text: Have you never— have you never heard of the Ku Klux Klan?
context: Holmes comments on the threatening orange pips during the investigation.

text: You will observe, that the sudden breaking up of the society was coincident with the disappearance of Openshaw from America with their papers. It may well have been cause and effect. It is no wonder that he and his family have some of the more implacable spirits upon their track. You can understand that this register and diary may implicate some of the first men in the South, and that there may be many who will not sleep easy at night until it is recovered.
context: Holmes comments on the threatening orange pips during the investigation.

text: Now, Watson, You'll come with me, won't you?
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating Neville St. Clair's disappearance.

text: We are on the outskirts of Lee, We have touched on three English counties in our short drive, starting in Middlesex, passing over an angle of Surrey, and ending in Kent. See that light among the trees? That is The Cedars, and beside that lamp sits a woman whose anxious ears have already, I have little doubt, caught the clink of our horse's feet.
context: Holmes comments on Neville St. Clair's disappearance during the investigation.

text: Coarse writing, Surely this is not your husband's writing, madam.
context: Holmes comments on Neville St. Clair's disappearance during the investigation.

text: It has been in some points a singular case, I confess that I have been as blind as a mole, but it is better to learn wisdom late than never to learn it at all.
context: Holmes comments on Neville St. Clair's disappearance during the investigation.

text: He certainly needs a wash, I had an idea that he might, and I took the liberty of bringing the tools with me.
context: Holmes comments on Neville St. Clair's disappearance during the investigation.

text: No crime, but a very great error has been committed, You would have done better to have trusted your wife.
context: Holmes comments on Neville St. Clair's disappearance during the investigation.

text: That was it, I have no doubt of it. But have you never been prosecuted for begging?
context: Holmes comments on Neville St. Clair's disappearance during the investigation.

text: I reached this one, by sitting upon five pillows and consuming an ounce of shag. I think, Watson, that if we drive to Baker Street we shall just be in time for breakfast.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating Neville St. Clair's disappearance.

text: No, no. No crime, Only one of those whimsical little incidents which will happen when you have four million human beings all jostling each other within the space of a few square miles. Amid the action and reaction of so dense a swarm of humanity, every possible combination of events may be expected to take place, and many a little problem will be presented which may be striking and bizarre without being criminal. We have already had experience of such.
context: Holmes comments on the stolen blue carbuncle during the investigation.

text: Here is my lens. You know my methods. What can you gather yourself as to the individuality of the man who has worn this article?
context: Holmes comments on the stolen blue carbuncle during the investigation.

text: On the contrary, Watson, you can see everything. You fail, however, to reason from what you see. You are too timid in drawing your inferences.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating the stolen blue carbuncle.

text: Eh? What of it, then? Has it returned to life and flapped off through the kitchen window?
context: Holmes comments on the stolen blue carbuncle during the investigation.

text: Hum! So much for the police-court, The question for us now to solve is the sequence of events leading from a rifled jewel-case at one end to the crop of a goose in Tottenham Court Road at the other. You see, Watson, our little deductions have suddenly assumed a much more important and less innocent aspect. Here is the stone; the stone came from the goose, and the goose came from Mr. Henry Baker, the gentleman with the bad hat and all the other characteristics with which I have bored you. So now we must set ourselves very seriously to finding this gentleman and ascertaining what part he has played in this little mystery.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating the stolen blue carbuncle.

text: To do this, we must try the simplest means first, and these lie undoubtedly in an advertisement in all the evening papers. If this fail, I shall have recourse to other methods.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating the stolen blue carbuncle.

text: We have retained these things for some days, because we expected to see an advertisement from you giving your address. I am at a loss to know now why you did not advertise.
context: Holmes comments on the stolen blue carbuncle during the investigation.

text: So much for Mr. Henry Baker, It is quite certain that he knows nothing whatever about the matter. Are you hungry, Watson?
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating the stolen blue carbuncle.

text: Well, I have no connection with any other people who have been making inquiries, If you won't tell us the bet is off, that is all. But I'm always ready to back my opinion on a matter of fowls, and I have a fiver on it that the bird I ate is country bred.
context: Holmes comments on the stolen blue carbuncle during the investigation.

text: Ha! this may save us a visit to Brixton Road, Come with me, and we will see what is to be made of this fellow.
context: Holmes comments on the stolen blue carbuncle during the investigation.

text: You will excuse me, but I could not help overhearing the questions which you put to the salesman just now. I think that I could be of assistance to you.
context: Holmes comments on the stolen blue carbuncle during the investigation.

text: No, no; the real name, It is always awkward doing business with an alias.
context: Holmes comments on the stolen blue carbuncle during the investigation.

text: Here we are! The fire looks very seasonable in this weather. You look cold, Mr. Ryder. Pray take the basket-chair. I will just put on my slippers before we settle this little matter of yours.
context: Holmes addresses a client while investigating the stolen blue carbuncle.

text: Now, then! You want to know what became of those geese?
context: Holmes addresses a client while investigating the stolen blue carbuncle.

text: The game's up, Ryder, Hold up, man, or you'll be into the fire! Give him an arm back into his chair, Watson. He's not got blood enough to go in for felony with impunity. Give him a dash of brandy. So!
context: Holmes addresses James Ryder while investigating the stolen blue carbuncle.

text: Now he looks a little more human. What a shrimp it is, to be sure!
context: Holmes addresses James Ryder while investigating the stolen blue carbuncle.

text: Get back into your chair! It is very well to cringe and crawl now, but you thought little enough of this poor Horner in the dock for a crime of which he knew nothing.
context: Holmes comments on the stolen blue carbuncle during the investigation.

text: After all, Watson, I am not retained by the police to supply their deficiencies. If Horner were in danger it would be another thing; but this fellow will not appear against him, and the case must collapse. I suppose that I am commuting a felony, but it is just possible that I am saving a soul. This fellow will not go wrong again; he is too terribly frightened. Send him to gaol now, and you make him a gaol-bird for life.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating the stolen blue carbuncle.

text: Besides, it is the season of forgiveness. Chance has put in our way a most singular and whimsical problem, and its solution is its own reward. If you will have the goodness to touch the bell, Doctor, we will begin another investigation, in which, also a bird will be the chief feature.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating the stolen blue carbuncle.

text: Good-morning, madam, My name is Sherlock Holmes. This is my intimate friend and associate, Dr. Watson, before whom you can speak as freely as before myself. Ha! I am glad to see that Mrs. Hudson has had the good sense to light the fire. Pray draw up to it, and I shall order you a cup of hot coffee, for I observe that you are shivering.
context: Holmes addresses a client while investigating Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran.

text: Indeed, Was it your custom always to lock yourselves in at night?
context: Holmes comments on Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran during the investigation.

text: One moment, are you sure about this whistle and metallic sound? Could you swear to it?
context: Holmes comments on Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran during the investigation.

text: You have done wisely, But have you told me all?
context: Holmes comments on Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran during the investigation.

text: And what do you think of it all, Watson?
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran.

text: He seems a very amiable person, I am not quite so bulky, but if he had remained I might have shown him that my grip was not much more feeble than his own.
context: Holmes comments on Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran during the investigation.

text: There is some building going on there, that is where we are going.
context: Holmes comments on Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran during the investigation.

text: And the lady, I fancy, is Miss Stoner, Yes, I think we had better do as you suggest.
context: Holmes addresses Helen Stoner while investigating Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran.

text: I thought it as well, that this fellow should think we had come here as architects, or on some definite business. It may stop his gossip. Good-afternoon, Miss Stoner. You see that we have been as good as our word.
context: Holmes comments on Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran during the investigation.

text: Do you know, Watson, I have really some scruples as to taking you to-night. There is a distinct element of danger.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran.

text: Subtle enough and horrible enough. When a doctor does go wrong he is the first of criminals. He has nerve and he has knowledge. Palmer and Pritchard were among the heads of their profession. This man strikes even deeper, but I think, Watson, that we shall be able to strike deeper still.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran.

text: But we shall have horrors enough before the night is over; for goodness' sake let us have a quiet pipe and turn our minds for a few hours to something more cheerful.
context: Holmes comments on Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran during the investigation.

text: That is our signal, it comes from the middle window.
context: Holmes comments on Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran during the investigation.

text: It means that it is all over, And perhaps, after all, it is for the best. Take your pistol, and we will enter Dr. Roylott's room.
context: Holmes comments on Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran during the investigation.

text: It is a swamp adder! the deadliest snake in India. He has died within ten seconds of being bitten. Violence does, in truth, recoil upon the violent, and the schemer falls into the pit which he digs for another. Let us thrust this creature back into its den, and we can then remove Miss Stoner to some place of shelter and let the county police know what has happened.
context: Holmes comments on Helen Stoner's danger at Stoke Moran during the investigation.

text: None at all, They are coiners on a large scale, and have used the machine to form the amalgam which has taken the place of silver.
context: Holmes comments on Victor Hatherley's account of the hydraulic press during the investigation.

text: Well, at least, you have had your revenge upon them. There can be no question that it was your oil-lamp which, when it was crushed in the press, set fire to the wooden walls, though no doubt they were too excited in the chase after you to observe it at the time. Now keep your eyes open in this crowd for your friends of last night, though I very much fear that they are a good hundred miles off by now.
context: Holmes comments on Victor Hatherley's account of the hydraulic press during the investigation.

text: Experience, Indirectly it may be of value, you know; you have only to put it into words to gain the reputation of being excellent company for the remainder of your existence.
context: Holmes comments on Victor Hatherley's account of the hydraulic press during the investigation.

text: Good-day, Lord St. Simon, Pray take the basket-chair. This is my friend and colleague, Dr. Watson. Draw up a little to the fire, and we will talk this matter over.
context: Holmes addresses Lord St. Simon while investigating Lady St. Simon's disappearance.

text: You can understand, that I extend to the affairs of my other clients the same secrecy which I promise to you in yours.
context: Holmes comments on Lady St. Simon's disappearance during the investigation.

text: Well, certainly that is also a conceivable hypothesis, And now, Lord St. Simon, I think that I have nearly all my data. May I ask whether you were seated at the breakfast-table so that you could see out of the window?
context: Holmes addresses Lord St. Simon while investigating Lady St. Simon's disappearance.

text: It is very good of Lord St. Simon to honour my head by putting it on a level with his own, I think that I shall have a whisky and soda and a cigar after all this cross-questioning. I had formed my conclusions as to the case before our client came into the room.
context: Holmes comments on Lady St. Simon's disappearance during the investigation.

text: Very good, Lestrade, You really are very fine indeed. Let me see it.
context: Holmes addresses Inspector Lestrade while investigating Lady St. Simon's disappearance.

text: Just one hint to you, Lestrade, I will tell you the true solution of the matter. Lady St. Simon is a myth. There is not, and there never has been, any such person.
context: Holmes addresses Inspector Lestrade while investigating Lady St. Simon's disappearance.

text: I think that I heard a ring, Yes, there are steps on the landing. If I cannot persuade you to take a lenient view of the matter, Lord St. Simon, I have brought an advocate here who may be more successful.
context: Holmes addresses Lord St. Simon while investigating Lady St. Simon's disappearance.

text: I had hoped, that you would have joined us in a friendly supper.
context: Holmes comments on Lady St. Simon's disappearance during the investigation.

text: Then I trust that you at least will honour me with your company, It is always a joy to meet an American, Mr. Moulton, for I am one of those who believe that the folly of a monarch and the blundering of a minister in far-gone years will not prevent our children from being some day citizens of the same world-wide country under a flag which shall be a quartering of the Union Jack with the Stars and Stripes.
context: Holmes comments on Lady St. Simon's disappearance during the investigation.

text: The case has been an interesting one, because it serves to show very clearly how simple the explanation may be of an affair which at first sight seems to be almost inexplicable. Nothing could be more natural than the sequence of events as narrated by this lady, and nothing stranger than the result when viewed, for instance, by Mr. Lestrade of Scotland Yard.
context: Holmes comments on Lady St. Simon's disappearance during the investigation.

text: Ah, Watson, perhaps you would not be very gracious either, if, after all the trouble of wooing and wedding, you found yourself deprived in an instant of wife and of fortune. I think that we may judge Lord St. Simon very mercifully and thank our stars that we are never likely to find ourselves in the same position. Draw your chair up and hand me my violin, for the only problem we have still to solve is how to while away these bleak autumnal evenings.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating Lady St. Simon's disappearance.

text: Pray compose yourself, sir, and let me have a clear account of who you are and what it is that has befallen you.
context: Holmes addresses a client while investigating the missing beryls from the coronet.

text: Now, my dear sir, is it not obvious to you now that this matter really strikes very much deeper than either you or the police were at first inclined to think? It appeared to you to be a simple case; to me it seems exceedingly complex. Consider what is involved by your theory. You suppose that your son came down from his bed, went, at great risk, to your dressing-room, opened your bureau, took out your coronet, broke off by main force a small portion of it, went off to some other place, concealed three gems out of the thirty-nine, with such skill that nobody can find them, and then returned with the other thirty-six into the room in which he exposed himself to the greatest danger of being discovered. I ask you now, is such a theory tenable?
context: Holmes addresses a client while investigating the missing beryls from the coronet.

text: It is our task to find that out, so now, if you please, Mr. Holder, we will set off for Streatham together, and devote an hour to glancing a little more closely into details.
context: Holmes addresses Alexander Holder while investigating the missing beryls from the coronet.

text: I fully share your opinion, and I trust, with you, that we may prove it, I believe I have the honour of addressing Miss Mary Holder. Might I ask you a question or two?
context: Holmes comments on the missing beryls from the coronet during the investigation.

text: He stood, to the left of the door—that is to say, farther up the path than is necessary to reach the door?
context: Holmes comments on the missing beryls from the coronet during the investigation.

text: Now, Mr. Holder, here is the corner which corresponds to that which has been so unfortunately lost. Might I beg that you will break it off.
context: Holmes addresses Alexander Holder while investigating the missing beryls from the coronet.

text: I think that we may safely say, that she is wherever Sir George Burnwell is. It is equally certain, too, that whatever her sins are, they will soon receive a more than sufficient punishment.
context: Holmes comments on the missing beryls from the coronet during the investigation.

text: To the man who loves art for its own sake, it is frequently in its least important and lowliest manifestations that the keenest pleasure is to be derived. It is pleasant to me to observe, Watson, that you have so far grasped this truth that in these little records of our cases which you have been good enough to draw up, and, I am bound to say, occasionally to embellish, you have given prominence not so much to the many _causes célèbres_ and sensational trials in which I have figured but rather to those incidents which may have been trivial in themselves, but which have given room for those faculties of deduction and of logical synthesis which I have made my special province.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating Violet Hunter's employment at the Copper Beeches.

text: No, it is not selfishness or conceit, If I claim full justice for my art, it is because it is an impersonal thing—a thing beyond myself. Crime is common. Logic is rare. Therefore it is upon the logic rather than upon the crime that you should dwell.
context: Holmes comments on Violet Hunter's employment at the Copper Beeches during the investigation.

text: And she would need to be, I am much mistaken if we do not hear from her before many days are past.
context: Holmes comments on Violet Hunter's employment at the Copper Beeches during the investigation.

text: Data! data! data! I can't make bricks without clay.
context: Holmes comments on Violet Hunter's employment at the Copper Beeches during the investigation.

text: They always fill me with a certain horror. It is my belief, Watson, founded upon my experience, that the lowest and vilest alleys in London do not present a more dreadful record of sin than does the smiling and beautiful countryside.
context: Holmes addresses Watson while investigating Violet Hunter's employment at the Copper Beeches.

text: I am glad of all details, whether they seem to you to be relevant or not.
context: Holmes comments on Violet Hunter's employment at the Copper Beeches during the investigation.

text: Pray continue, Your narrative promises to be a most interesting one.
context: Holmes comments on Violet Hunter's employment at the Copper Beeches during the investigation.

text: You have done well indeed! Now lead the way, and we shall soon see the end of this black business.
context: Holmes comments on Violet Hunter's employment at the Copper Beeches during the investigation.

text: There has been some villainy here, this beauty has guessed Miss Hunter's intentions and has carried his victim off.
context: Holmes comments on Violet Hunter's employment at the Copper Beeches during the investigation.

text: Ha! It is clear that Mrs. Toller knows more about this matter than anyone else.
context: Holmes comments on Violet Hunter's employment at the Copper Beeches during the investigation.

text: Ah, I think that what you have been good enough to tell us makes the matter fairly clear, and that I can deduce all that remains. Mr. Rucastle then, I presume, took to this system of imprisonment?
context: Holmes comments on Violet Hunter's employment at the Copper Beeches during the investigation.

text: I am sure we owe you an apology, Mrs. Toller, for you have certainly cleared up everything which puzzled us. And here comes the country surgeon and Mrs. Rucastle, so I think, Watson, that we had best escort Miss Hunter back to Winchester, as it seems to me that our _locus standi_ now is rather a questionable one.
context: Holmes addresses Watson and Mrs. Toller while investigating Violet Hunter's employment at the Copper Beeches.
